ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general hot chocolate beverage class; no specific oat-milk hot chocolate entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers chocolate-flavoured drinks broadly; no oat-milk-specific subcode exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for oat-milk hot chocolate beverage; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: may contain traces of tree nuts depending on oat milk brand

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard café serving of oatmilk hot chocolate is approximately one medium cup (355 ml / 355 g), equivalent to a 12 fl oz café-style drink.

nutrient_source.title: Oatmilk Signature Hot Chocolate — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (G00010296)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo record G00010296 for Oatmilk Signature Hot Chocolate. All per-100g values are as reported. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients are not reported in the HPB record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; oat milk and chocolate-flavoured milk beverages as analogous reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific oatmilk hot chocolate product. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on: (1) oat milk GI ~60 per University of Sydney GI Database (oat-based beverages), (2) added mocha/vanilla syrup raises effective GI toward the medium-high boundary due to high sugar content (9.94g/100g). GL per serving calculated as: GI (60) × carbohydrate per serving (36.8g for 355g serving) / 100 = 22.1. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.94g/100g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite low sodium (3mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this beverage due to its high sugar load (9.94g/100g), which independently raises cardiovascular and blood pressure risk; request an unsweetened oat milk option with unsweetened cocoa powder instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.94g sugar/100g and zero dietary fibre triggers the SSB hard gate for type 2 diabetes; liquid sugars are rapidly absorbed, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its high sugar content (9.94g/100g), negligible fibre (0g/100g), and medium-to-high glycemic index (~60); it should be avoided or replaced with an unsweetened oat milk cocoa preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar content (9.94g/100g) in a liquid beverage contributes to elevated triglycerides, insulin resistance, and cardiovascular risk; fat content is low (0.25g/100g) and sodium is minimal (3mg/100g).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this beverage due to its high sugar content (9.94g/100g), which promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and metabolic dysfunction; the low fat and sodium profile are positive but insufficient to offset the sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar content (9.94g/100g) promotes systemic inflammation relevant to RA disease activity; no clinically meaningful anti-inflammatory factors are present in this sweetened preparation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage due to its pro-inflammatory sugar load (9.94g/100g) and absence of anti-inflammatory factors; unsweetened oat milk with unsweetened cocoa would restore beta-glucan and polyphenol benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oatmilk Signature Hot Chocolate is a café-style hot beverage made with steamed oat milk combined with mocha-flavoured and vanilla-flavoured syrups. It is a dairy-free, plant-based alternative to traditional hot chocolate, popular in Singapore café settings. The drink has a smooth, creamy texture from the steamed oat milk and a sweet chocolate flavour profile derived primarily from the added syrups rather than pure cocoa. Per 100g, it provides 42 kcal, 0g protein, 0.25g fat, and 10.36g carbohydrates of which 9.94g is sugar, with negligible dietary fibre (0g) and very low sodium (3mg). The high sugar content (9.94g/100g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), making it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 60 (medium) based on oat milk beverage analogues, with a glycemic load of approximately 22 per standard 355ml serving. Despite oat milk being a healthier dairy alternative with natural beta-glucan fibre, the added chocolate and vanilla syrups dominate the nutritional profile, negating the fibre benefit. This beverage is vegan, dairy-free, and halal-suitable but contains gluten from oats. It is best consumed occasionally and in moderation by healthy individuals; those with metabolic conditions should request unsweetened alternatives.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo canonical record G00010296 with confidence 0.95. All core macros are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database oat milk beverage category, clearly documented.
notes: protein_g is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record. This is plausible for a dilute syrup-and-oat-milk beverage where protein from oat milk is highly diluted; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo G00010296. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo G00010296 — the oat milk beta-glucan fibre is effectively absent at the dilution level of this prepared beverage. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dairy-free beverage; naturally zero. magnesium_mg is 0.0 per HPB source; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo G00010296. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB Foodo G00010296. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in HPB Foodo G00010296. GI value of 60 is an analogous estimate based on oat milk beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database; direct measurement for this specific product is not available. GL per serving = 60 × (10.36 × 355/100) / 100 = 60 × 36.78 / 100 = 22.1. This is a Western café-style beverage sold in Singapore; however, as per G6, all Singaporean dishes require multilingual names. Therefore, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names have been added.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per validator feedback.

Added mandatory local-language names (Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) to `local_names` and `aliases_by_language` as this is a beverage sold in Singapore, thus falling under the Singaporean dish multilingual requirement (G6).
Ensured all new local-language names were also added to the flat `aliases` array to maintain consistency (q8).
Updated the `notes` field to reflect the inclusion of multilingual names and the rationale for their inclusion based on G6.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
